<@!593160154376044594> Please do not try to make the buttons or other labels scale with the text. It's really hard to build a layout around such behavior, and especially in table layouts, you are breaking essential optimizations in the Noesis internal layout algorithms. They ought to be fixed size, not `minWidth`, but fixed in both width and height. Use a `Viewbox` where absolutely required, in order to dynamically reduce the text size inside a label, should it not fit. Otherwise just cut the overflow, and adjust the translation to fit the space.

For elements with `IsVirtualizing`, fixed sizes are absolutely mandatory, but even in other parts of the UI it's a big factor in layout performance.

content: Doable, but that requires a very well designed icons that leave no space for interpretation. Human brain is a lazy organ. Making it struggle to understand stuff only works as disadvantage. If you can guarantee that all the functions will be understood by ALL, STRAIGHT AWAY, then this is the way. I'd use this method cautiously. It is far better to use labels because it gives exact meaning.
Your example shows a made-up word. What would be the longest word, in any language, that means 'construction', 'building'?
The translation of "Build" is either the verb "Construire", either the noun "Construction". I selected "Construction" as it's the most common I'm used to in games I played.
Of course, "Constructionsdfmkjsdlksdgflkhsd" is not a real word. That was kind of a length test. Same for the "Agriculture" translation in German, as they often have long words.

The question arose at first when all width were bound to the LBC4 "Designation" (En) lengthest button.
Translation is "Zones", the (Fr) near-shortest text, the hud did not accepted that very gracefully. I then changed the way "squaring" worked to be min-width=height-based, but that was then opposed to max-width=height also.

And now, here we are. I'm asking if relaxing the max width would be a valid option, and the UI constraints that is bound to that, likely what if all texts in a certain language were double the actual english length, and so how would a UI expert handle that.
Relaxing the max width would be the best solution. Preferably keeping all buttons the same width for consistency. Try also shrinking the text. Have it 10pt instead of 12pt, for example.

Unfortunately here there are buttons spanning from left and from right, so there's always that problem of overlapping interfaces. You might want to set a MAX WIDTH regardless. Maybe resolution divided by amount of buttons, in percentage?
Option of last resort is to re-design the information hierarchy to lessen the amount of initial buttons.
So that instead of 6 on left and 5 on right you end up with 4 on left and 3 on right (as an example)

Actually less worried about the space the German translation would require, there are plenty of non-literal translations fitting, plus the chance of using anglicisms (nouns, verbs and even abbreviations imported from English) when space is a concern.

French is almost as bad as it gets already, only surpassed by Italian, unless I'm mistaken, and neither of these can lean on vocabulary imported from a different language family for choice of synonyms. Arabic is also quite bad, but luckily usually completely irrelevant.
https://www.w3.org/International/articles/article-text-size.en

Easiness of hit is usually the leading parameter. The bigger the button, the easier it is to click. This is common to mobile apps too. Desktop buttons have the benefit of pinpoint mouse cursor so they can, generally, be a bit smaller. You don't want to make them tiny though.

Other thing is how close are the buttons to each other, especially in a chain flow. Let's say you select _Construction -> Workshops -> Wood_. If the consecutive buttons are close to each other, it is much easier, and quicker!, for user/player to follow the journey and ultimately get to its end.

<@!520653029955862528> Sprite definitions are spread over two (or technically 3) tables. `BaseSprites::SourceRectangle` tells you *where* to sample *from*, while `Sprites::Offset` then tells you how to align that in the default 64x32 space of a tile. `Items::spriteid` then indexes into `Sprites`, not `BaseSprites`.

You will have to do a join from `Sprites` onto `BaseSprites` to get the values you actually need. You will then also need to use the CSS property `clip-path` to correctly apply the boundaries of the source rectangle (from offset + source rectangle), and calculate the real paint offset from `Sprites::Offset - BaseSprites::SourceRectangle`.

I'm also making the assumption that QTs reflection system is similar to Unreals.

QObject comes with quite a lot of overhead, and you are not exactly supposed to have hundred thousands of them alive concurrently. If you aim at the "reflections" provided by the property system in Qt, you even have to go the route via string based lookups at runtime, nothing you would actually want to do.

Unless I'm mistaken, part of what makes the `CreatureType` enum currently "necessary", is that the inheritance structure in the whole creatures tree wasn't exactly fully thought through.   Like the distinction between "monster" and "animal", that should not even exist in the first place. The behavior tree should differ, but not the C++ type.
And it used a type hierarchy where it should have used entity-component pattern

E.g. components to plug in the specialized anatomy (damage, needs/upkeep, reproduction), movement constraints, can-work extension, combat-/social-stance (squad based, monster squad, ant swarm, regular animal).

Qts reflection system uses strings for runtime lookups? yikes.  Unreal provides a pointer to a class type object. After I typed that out I also saw that items extended from object as well which your totally right would be way to heavy for all of them to be QObjects.  I agree the component pattern should definitely be something to move towards. Again though you would still need some kind of reflection to grab a type of component otherwise your writing a function for each component and/or need an enum to check for the correct type to access it.  
```
//something that could look like this and be one generic function
getComponent<CanMove>()
getComponent<damage>()

//will look like this
GetCanMoveComponent()
GetDamageComponent()

//Another example with but using an enum as type. but this would be unsafe as passing in wrong types would cause an incorrect runtime cast
getComponent<CanMoveComponent>(ComponentType::CanMove)
```
I'm doing something similiar in the pull requrest I just made. 

You could also use dynamic_casts and get similar results, you could have a generic function that could  look like getComponent<CanMove>() but dynamic_casts are fairly slow if your doing a bunch of them. And unnecessary if your already storing an enum type with the class.
There is no need to use a generic base class for components though, is there? It's not like we would have to design an entity-component *framework*, but we can use typed member pointers.

Also, even though i said its not hard to implement it still would take time to refactor the existing code to work like this. I think giving them a generic base component and then making them members variables like you said is the best first step.

few things that you might or might not like:
1. This first set of changes just removes `QSet` completely, because it was the easiest
2. I've changed the C++ version to C++20, because the STL has improved a lot on that version, it makes things easier without extra libs. This can be reverted to 17, but things will have to be changed (for this first step, `std::set` will not have a `contains()` anymore)
3. Because of this, Linux now compiles with GCC 10 (instead of the previous GCC 7)

Beware of some performance differences between STL and the Qt containers. Copying (nested) Qt structures at the message queue boundary is a lazy, shallow copy-on-write. With the STL containers it's an eager deep copy.
Also found the performance of `std:: unordered_*` containers to be lacking compared to the Qt ones - need to switch over to `absl::flat_hash_*` in some places.
Yeah, this is just a first pass, it's easier using the STL, but then performance analysis can be done, should be easy to change to specific ones later

Pondering what to use for the message queue...
Leaning towards  `std::unique_ptr<std::function<void(void)>>` as the base container template, but still pondering how much flexibility I should account for with regard to coupling specific sender/receiver pairs.

As long as they are pairwise, I believe I can just bind the receiver directly from the senders thread, and I can completely forgo serialization for now?

Just need to make sure the sender provides data to `std::bind` strictly as rvalues, and the receiver works strictly with lvalue references.
I don't think we will ever need n:1 or 1:n connections on the message passing end.
The receiving end requires a base class for providing push access to the correct queue....
Got to be careful to only set up signal/slot connections after construction in the correct thread so not to risk any races.
And in reverse also the detail about destruction in the correct thread - which mustn't be on the sender but on the receivers end - after the sender discards it's (only?) strong ref.
The sender can't own the receiver directly, it can at most own a reference counter. On expiring, full ownership needs to be transferred to the target queue, and needs to be released there.
The construction should already happen in the correct thread as well... However, if I were to use a future here to obtain the receiver object for binding, there is a risk of deadlocks.
Damn, not that easy to just replace the comfort Qt gave:/

Maybe protobuf and going for a generic buffer only interface between sender and receiver with a factory service (including remote destruction) on the simulation side is easier to implement. I don't think I'm going to be happy for long with having to manage the corresponding other threads pointers.
Factory service which doubles as router also reliefs me of the question where the injected aggregators were supposed to get the simulation objects from for setting up their connections.
With a factory service, the UI needs to specify a unique topic for distinction...

There is some really weird details I don't like, such as `SpriteSeason` having a nested `SpriteSeasonRotation`, but that missing a `Sprite` key e.g. Or `BaseSprite` / `Sprite` not being strictly `oneof` within the different compositions. Some dead flags, such as `Sprite::Rot90`, `Sprite::Anim`, `Sprite::HasRandom` or `Sprite::HasTransp` which should had been derived from the actual content / sprite hierarchy.
`Effect: "none"` in `SpriteByMaterials::Effect` indicates a missing optional
Valid effects should be an enum, `Rot90, FlipHorizontal`.

The implicit convention that a sprite can *not* have a `BaseSprite` defined on the composition options (in which case it implicitly inherits the `Sprite::BaseSprite` respectively if that is also missing then `Sprite::key`) is something we should not allow, I believe?

Hrm... we do have that kind of abstraction anyway. If you take a look at what a savegame includes, you will find that `sprites.json` which maps `(ItemSID, MaterialSIDs, Random)` to the `uint32_t` IDs used exclusively in the simulation for bookkeeping.

\o I'm working on a similar style game that I'm making for N64 hardware, quick question to see if I understand the GNU license.

I would say probably. Are you trying to close source your project?
Also, this sort of game requires a lot of processing power and RAM for older hardware. There is a lot of pathfinding running constantly and a lot of items to keep track of.

Has anyone been looking into implementing Claude code or alt. AI for the sake of modding? Currently I’m putting together a piecemeal build of gnomoria with the aims of making a 2.0. With the help of Claude it will be much easier as a side project versus having to do full implementations after work.

I love this game and there is so much it could have been but I really just want a non-broken vanilla that is able to be played end game
I’d love to work together on something, especially if you have some experience as I have only ever coded college-level practice projects

Remember that if you choose to go the vibe coding route instead of actually learning and understanding, you will end up in situations where you have a problem that needs to be solved, but you cannot solve it because the AI keeps going around in circles not doing what you tell it to do. It's happened a thousand times before to people who built entire SaaS'es around vibe coding, and it will happen to you.
I will also caution that you have blind spots, and Claud WILL put bugs in them while not helping you descover them.
I’m aware of what comes with relying on AI to do intensive work. I respect the input but im more so looking to see if anyone is doing similar.

I don't think you'll find anyone in here. The server is pretty inactive, and from what I've seen the gamedev (and gamer) community as a whole leans heavily against AI. As does pretty much every creative community. So you'd have much more luck trying some reddit or discord community dedicated specifically for AI. Like ones for OpenClaw or Google Genie or r/vibecoding.
As for the art assets, there are plenty of tutorials on local GenAI in the Stable Diffusion discord (and the Furry Diffusion one especially). And Aseprite also have several local AI plugins to let you generate and work with sprites (like [PixelAI](https://red335.itch.io/pixelai-local-ai-directly-in-aseprite) or [Retro Diffusion](https://astropulse.itch.io/retrodiffusion)).
